description: The horse: strength and speed lent willingly or not — the plow's partner, the road's engine, the fable's laborer. Named by sound rather than family: the word strides. A counted horse may take optional 'lipha'.
sound_symbolism: A hard 'ka' strikes off, 'lo' rolls through the stride, and open 'ra' finishes at a run — hoofbeat rhythm at speaking pace, which is the only speed Phi permits even its fastest animal.
grammatical_notes: Noun: 'kalora reshi rashelo.' (The horse runs fast.) From Schleicher's fable: 'ta kalora tumoa rolutha to natu.' (One horse pulled a heavy wagon.) The fable is worth reading for what Phi's grammar does to the horses' complaint.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Before engines, the horse was the engine: field, road, and mill all turned on its patience. Every pre-industrial mile owed it something.
